articles.title: Daytime Napping and Mortality Association in Older Adults
articles.summary: A recent study found an association between longer and more frequent daytime napping and higher mortality risk. The authors suggest using napping frequency and duration as behavioral markers of increased mortality risk […]

articles.title: Obesity’s Effects on the Immune System May Linger for Years
articles.summary: A new study has suggested that T cells might retain a pro-inflammatory phenotype long after normal weight is regained following a period of obesity. In mice, the effect lasts for […]

articles.title: A Robust Senescence Response Helps Wounds Heal
articles.summary: A team of scientists has examined how younger and older mice heal from wounds and found that more robust senescent cell activation in younger animals helps them heal faster. A double-edged sword […]

articles.title: AI-powered forecasts sharpen early warning for destructive crop pest
articles.summary: What if farmers could see a pest outbreak coming before the insect ever had a chance to damage their crop? New research from Texas A&M AgriLife Research indicates that artificial intelligence can predict outbreaks much more accurately than traditional methods. The tool could dramatically improve how and when insect pest risks are identified and controlled.

articles.title: TxPert: using multiple knowledge graphs for prediction of transcriptomic perturbation effects
articles.summary: Nature Biotechnology, Published online: 01 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41587-026-03113-4 TxPert predicts unseen single transcriptomic perturbations with accuracy approaching split-half experimental reproducibility.

articles.title: DNA-guided CRISPR–Cas12a effectors for programmable RNA recognition and cleavage
articles.summary: Nature Biotechnology, Published online: 01 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41587-026-03120-5 Synthetic DNA guides (crDNA) reprogram Cas12a nucleases for RNA targeting.

articles.title: CRISPR speed patterns can identify multiple viruses and variants simultaneously
articles.summary: As the spread of infectious diseases accelerates, technologies that can accurately distinguish multiple viruses in a single test are becoming increasingly important. KAIST and an international research team have developed a new diagnostic technology that simultaneously identifies various viruses and variants by controlling the "speed" of gene scissors.

articles.title: Gene circuits reshape DNA folding and affect how genes are expressed, study finds
articles.summary: When a gene is turned on in a cell, it creates a ripple effect along the DNA strand, changing the physical structure of the strand. A new study by MIT researchers, appearing in Science, shows that these ripples can stimulate or suppress neighboring genes. These effects, which result from the winding or unwinding of neighboring DNA, are determined by the order of genes along a strand of DNA. Genes upstream of the active gene are usually turned up, while those downstream are inhibited.

articles.title: Autumn leaves transformed into biodegradable mulch film can curb farm plastic pollution
articles.summary: Fallen leaves, which are discarded every year, have been transformed into a resource that can replace waste plastics, a major nuisance in rural areas. A research team at the Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST) has developed biodegradable agricultural vinyl made from fallen leaves, presenting a new way to solve the problem of conventional plastic vinyl, which is a cause of soil pollution. The study is published in the journal Green Chemistry.

articles.title: Light-activated protein illuminates when embryos can cope with disruptions to cell division
articles.summary: Cell division during the early stage of embryo development is a trade-off between speed and accuracy; the cells need to divide quickly to enable rapid growth, but it's important not to introduce errors that could be fatal to the developing embryo. But some stages of embryo development might be more tolerant of errors, coping with them without leading to complete failure.

articles.title: Water molecules found to actively drive gene transcription process
articles.summary: Researchers have uncovered a previously hidden layer of complexity in how genes are activated, showing that water molecules play a direct and essential role in one of the most fundamental processes in biology: DNA transcription.

articles.title: A silent robot shadows sperm whales by listening to their clicks
articles.summary: An autonomous underwater glider is giving us a new and effective way to track sperm whales by tuning into their clicks and silently following them. To study these large oceanic predators, researchers need to monitor their movements and social interactions for months at a time. But that's not easy, because they swim deep and stay underwater for long periods, making them hard to reach.

articles.title: Assessing the impact of drones on whale sharks
articles.summary: In recent years, using drones for wildlife research has proven to be a valuable tool in collecting data for population surveys, observing behavior and measuring animals' physical dimensions. A new study led by Murdoch University has found that drones flown above the ocean are unlikely to disturb whale sharks, the world's largest fish. The findings are published in the journal Ecosphere.

articles.title: Bitcoin logs best monthly performance in 12 months during April
articles.summary: Despite being the best-performing month in the past 12 months, it still came in slightly below its historical average, according to CoinGlass data.

articles.title: US CISA adds ‘insane’ Linux Copy Fail flaw to watch list
articles.summary: Malicious actors with code execution capability may gain root access on Linux systems using as few as 10 lines of Python, according to a researcher.

articles.title: BlackRock urges OCC to drop tokenized reserve cap idea, expand eligible assets in GENIUS Act comment letter
articles.summary: The world's largest asset manager opposed a potential 20% cap on tokenized reserve assets, a constraint that would limit products like its BUIDL fund.

articles.title: CryptoQuant says bitcoin’s April price surge was ‘speculative’ as spot demand remains weak, warns of correction risk
articles.summary: Bitcoin's price rose about 20% in April, but the rally was driven by speculative perpetual futures demand while spot demand remained weak, CryptoQuant said.

articles.title: Leading Iranian crypto exchange Nobitex was founded by sons of elite political family tied to supreme leaders: Reuters
articles.summary: Reuters found that hundreds of millions of dollars in transactions tied to sanctioned Iranian state entities have moved through Nobitex since 2018.

articles.title: Brazil's central bank bans stablecoin and crypto settlement in cross-border payments
articles.summary: The ban applies to fintechs and payment firms, closing the back-end payment rail for cross-border flows, but individual crypto investors can still buy and hold assets.

articles.title: Crypto industry backs CLARITY Act yield compromise, pushes Senate Banking for markup
articles.summary: The agreement necessitates firms restructure reward programs from a "buy and hold" to a "buy and use" model; however, CCI raised concerns over its broad prohibition.

articles.title: Bitcoin's 'hazardous' airdrop: Why developers are warning against Paul Sztorc’s eCash fork
articles.summary: Developers and industry figures say the eCash proposal introduces user risk, uneven distribution and philosophical tension.

articles.title: Coinbase says deal reached on Clarity Act stablecoin yield, clearing path to long-stalled Senate markup
articles.summary: Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong urged the Senate Banking Committee to "mark it up" as a months-long stalemate appears to be at an end.

articles.title: Here’s what happened in crypto today
articles.summary: Need to know what happened in crypto today? Here is the latest news on daily trends and events impacting Bitcoin price, blockchain, DeFi, NFTs, Web3 and crypto regulation.
